step_by_step_thinking: I agree with agent1. While calcium gluconate, an insulin drip, and salbutamol nebulization effectively help shift potassium into cells or bind free potassium ions, magnesium sulfate (MgSO4) primarily serves as support during life-threatening arrhythmias caused by severe hyperkalemia without actively managing the elevated potassium level. Therefore, my answer remains unchanged: D - MgSO4. The additional point supporting this view is its role specifically in treating complications like ECG changes indicative of impending cardiac arrest due to high potassium, rather than reducing plasma potassium concentration directly. This makes it less relevant compared to other listed interventions aimed at correcting hyperkalemia itself.
